habitat: Antonelli Pond is a small fresh water body located about 50 meters from the UCSC Coastal Sciences Campus entrance. The area surrounding the specimen consisted of dense herbs, shrubs, and some riparian willows. It was about 15-20 meters from the water's margin. The area receives a moderate amount of light with a few medium sized willows nearby. The temperature was 61 degrees Fahrenheit
verbatimAttributes: Specimen sat about 6 inches off the ground. It had pinnate compound leaves that were deeply divided. The flower had 4 equal petals, 6 stamens (4 long, 2 short). The petals were purplish- white with dark veins visible.
locality: Observed at Antonelli Pond located about 50 meters east of the UCSC Coastal Sciences Campus entrance. Approached the pond from Delaware Ave through the eastern most entrance. The specimen was located in a small area on the western side of the path about 50 meters from the entrance. It lined the west and east side of the path. Specimen collected about 2 feet form path's edge.

The surrounding area is open meadows, and further away, a neighborhood of houses.

locality: Directly across from upper Village/Farm bus stop on Hagar Drive

habitat: Disturbed area. Grassland. Generally-South-facing slope and large field of flowers.
verbatimAttributes: Annual or biennial. Seems to hybridize. Variable flower color, this one purple with dark veins. 4 petals. Pinnately lobed leaves with dentate margins.
locality: Sloped field between Great Meadow Bike Path and Village Rd., approx. 85 ft. W of UCSC Village Housing Office. Overlooks intersection of Farm Road and Ranch View Road.

habitat: Disturbed soil on a sunny hilltop.
verbatimAttributes: Plant covered in stiff, sharp, sparse hairs. Flowers white, cruciform, with contrasting venation on petals.
locality: Top of hill above village, ~50 ft from dirt bike path.

habitat: grassland
verbatimAttributes: The field was full of tall grasses and had several of this specimens species nearby it in flower. Most of the flowers were white but a few were purple.
locality: near the stairs going from opers to the village

habitat: Growing in outskirts of parking lot against the chain link fence. Sunny, open area.
verbatimAttributes: Many individuals growing in open sunny area. Leaves hairy and pinnately lobed/dissected. Flowers are pink with dark veins and corolla has 4 petals. Fruit is a silique. Long, thick taproot present. Herbaceous.
locality: East most corner of East Remote Lot, south of Lower East Field.

habitat: Exposed grasslands with nearby coastal scrub
verbatimAttributes: Solitary bush with white and/or purple flowers and dry siliques. An exotic invasive
associatedTaxa: Stipa pulchra, Achillea millefolium
locality: At the Southeast corner of Younger Lagoon Natural Reserve, just North of the walking path

habitat: Younger Lagoon Beach
verbatimAttributes: Wild radish. Very small plant located next to other native plants. Green in color. Not very common on beach, and much smaller than mainland plants. Typically isolated and most likely from mainland seeds that strayed onto the beach.
associatedTaxa: Calystegia soldanella, Ambrosia chamissonis
locality: 20 meters directly west from entrance to Younger Lagoon Beach

habitat: Sandy/muddy soil, native and invasive grasses fill field. Near a highly disturbed walking trail. About 20m off sea cliffs. Located in a coastal prairie restoration area.
verbatimAttributes: Invasive, non-native. Plant has reddish brown herbaceous stem. Leaves are lobed and serrated. Four petals white flowers with 4 long, 2 short stamen. Has silique fruit.
associatedTaxa: Hordeum brachantherum, Danthonia californica, Lupinus arboreus.
locality: To the east of the Seymour Center on the Younger Lagoon Natural Reserve. Located 10m behind the first bench along the trail.

habitat: Soil was primarily sandy loam. The site was formerly agricultural land and is now being restored to grassland, scrub, and seasonal wetland habitats. The ground was relatively flat. Very sunny at times, but coastal fog occasionally shaded the area. The site was in close proximity to the ocean.
verbatimAttributes: White to pink petals; in some areas pink to purple stems and veins; thick tuberous root; herb; escaped cultivation.
locality: Found the specimen between the main walking path next to the Long Marine Lab which runs parallel to McAllister Way and the eastern walking path which runs parallel to the wall of the adjacent housing community.

habitat: Soil was primarily sandy loam. Formerly agricultural land being restored to grassland, scrub, and seasonal wetland habitats. The ground was relatively flat. Very sunny at times, but coastal fog occasionally shaded the area. The site was in close proximity to the ocean.
verbatimAttributes: White to pink petals; in some areas pink to purples stems and veins; thick tuberous root; herb; escaped cultivation.
locality: We found the specimen located between the main walking path next to the Long Marine Lab which runs parallel to McAllister Way and the eastern walking path which runs parallel to the wall of the adjacent housing community.

habitat: Developed areas with preserved coastal shrubland.
locality: End of McCalister way near the Seymour Center parking lot.

habitat: Maritime chaparral, full sun and exposure to winds.
verbatimAttributes: Medium sized plant with purple and white flowers on long stalks. Common name: wild radish
locality: Head NW on Delaware Ave to CBB. Plant collected near NOAA Fisheries Laboratory.

habitat: Younger Lagoon Reserve Terrace Lands
verbatimAttributes: Wild Radish. Pinnate venation in petals. Petals white to purple on edges. Stamen and carpel extending past petals.
associatedTaxa: field mustard, sourgrass, yarrow
locality: 50 feet S of Southwest Fisheries Science Center

habitat: Restored agricultural lands. Coastal scrub bordered by coastal grasslands. Abundant sun and flat aspect. Area moderately disturbed with foot traffic.
verbatimAttributes: Flower has 4 petals with 2 short and 4 long stamens. Leaves and stem sparsely hairy and leaves pinnately divided. Stem is purple.
associatedTaxa: Coyote brush, Sticky monkey flower, Bermuda buttercup, California sagebrush, various other herbs and grasses.
locality: From entrance of UCSC coastal campus, follow path to the south towards the ocean for roughly 150m. Then move into the section of the Terrance Lands on the west side of the path.

occurrenceRemarks: common in disturbed soil of roadside and adjacent flats

locality: Sacramento Valley, East Main Street (Co. Rd. 22), Woodland (parallel to I-5), 1.7 mile west of the Sacramento River
